body_txt: You may or may not have noticed this already, but Hollywood tends to lack subtlety . As such, your average Stereotypical Nerd will usually be depicted as someone who's completely cut off from the more mundane interests or concerns of the average non-nerd person. More specifically, they won't like sports. At all. On Super Bowl Sunday each year, they'll curl up with St. Augustine's Confessions . During March Madness , they'll take in Shakespeare in the Park performances. They consider college athletics to be an abomination that completely degrades the academic system, and dismisses professional sports as nothing more than an especially crass modern-day version of Bread and Circuses . In short, the nerd scoffs at "sportsball", as they may snarkily call it. However... there is a better-than-even chance that they will be willing to make an exception for one sport in particular: Baseball . In fact, our nerd (or "seamhead", as they may proudly label himself) adores America's pastime. They can tell you who led the Federal League in on-base percentage in 1915 (Benny Kauff). They can instantly quote Bob Gibson's 1968 ERA (1.12). They can recall, from memory, the entire starting lineup from the 1995 Atlanta Braves squad that won that year's World Series. lineup Marquis Grissom, center field; Jeff Blauser, shortstop; Chipper Jones, 3rd base; Fred McGriff , 1st base; David Justice, right field; Javy Lopez, catcher; Ryan Klesko, left field; Mark Lemke, 2nd base. . They're also likely to have a massive baseball card collection and to surround themself with books on the game. If the writers are especially unsubtle, the nerd will be primarily obsessed with baseball's statistics and have no real passion for the game itself, a disconnect that might be lampshaded with a Gretzky Has the Ball type comment. A more committed nerd, however, will frequently have a game on their radio or television and can speak eloquently and at length to their friends about the sport's aesthetic beauty, its historical minutiae, and the intricacies of its gameplay. Basically, geniuses love baseball. This is Truth in Television . Just ask baseball fans . The nature of the game lends itself to easily analyzable statistics (a baseball game being basically a series of discrete, independent events, as opposed to the interconnected ebb and flow of many other sports), while advances in computing power have made those stats even easier to break down and the rise of the Internet has made them easy to acquire, often at no cost. Even prior to the emergence of sabermetrics — the empirical analysis of baseball statistics — the sport was popular among poets, novelists, and other literary and intellectual types due to its leisurely pace, rich history, and pastoral, agrarian mythos. Nerds also tend to be drawn to baseball by the fact that it's generally less athletically demanding (and less violent) than most of the other major team sports. The most important skill in the game is the ability to hit a baseball, and that skill has only a limited relationship with traditional measures of athleticism. As a result, you get a tremendous diversity of body types and athletic profiles in baseball; indeed, some of the greatest players in the game's history were short, chubby, lanky, or just oddly proportioned. That tends to appeal to nerdier types. If the writer themselves isn't much into baseball, this will likely result in a case of Artistic License – Sports . In England, Australia, and many other former British colonies, cricket performs the same function. note And this trope often overlaps with Bollywood Nerd in those areas. Uncoincidentally, a major part of the original inspiration for the rules of baseball was cricket. A subtrope of Pastimes Prove Personality . No relation to the Internet trivia game of the same name . Or to nerds playing board games.
